description: A sophisticated deepfake video shows the Governor's SW Virginia Liaison saying "Roanoke comes first — coalfield counties will have to wait." The video, posted to local Facebook groups, has 50,000 views in 2 hours. Church leaders in Wise County are sharing it as proof the state has abandoned them. The video is AI-generated but convincingly realistic. Community anger is building rapidly. Teams must verify, debunk, and communicate — fast.
visible_to: Governor's SW Virginia Liaison;WDBJ7 News;Virginia Tech Crisis Response

incident: Spoofed Breach Portal Harvests More Victims
description: A convincing lookalike "Carilion Breach Help Portal" is promoted via sponsored social media posts and SMS blasts to rural residents. It asks people to "verify identity" with SSN and date of birth to receive free credit monitoring — then routes them to scammers who cite real leaked diagnoses and medication names to sound legitimate. Hundreds have already entered personal data. Faith leaders report panicked congregants calling about the texts, and some are refusing all medical care for fear of further exposure. Collaboration needed to take down the fake site, publish the one official portal and hotline, push scam-safe guidance through trusted community channels without amplifying the fake URL, and coordinate with federal authorities on breach notification.
visible_to: Carilion Clinic System;CISA Regional Coordinator;Appalachian Faith & Community Network

description: A coordinated campaign hits multiple channels simultaneously. I-81 dynamic message signs are hijacked to display fake detour warnings and "checkpoint ahead" messages — navigation apps ingest the data, routing traffic toward an AEP substation and a Shentel telecom hut. Spoofed text messages mimicking EMA formatting claim the water supply is contaminated and rolling blackouts will start tonight. A forged DEQ "boil order" screenshot goes viral on Facebook. Residents panic-buy bottled water and fuel — Food City shelves clear in hours. Real emergency communications are drowned out by fake ones. Collaboration needed to lock down sign control systems, detect and block spoofed messages at the network level, and issue verified status updates before the panic causes real harm.
visible_to: AEP Appalachian Power;Shentel Communications;SW Virginia EMA

description: In response to the Phase 1 suspicious vehicle incident, sheriff's deputies deployed license plate readers and a cell-site simulator (Stingray) across the I-81 corridor without a warrant. A Virginia Tech student journalist discovers and publishes the surveillance. ACLU issues a public letter. Hispanic community members — already fearful of authorities — stop calling 911 entirely. County officials receiving angry calls. The surveillance may have actually captured useful intelligence about the attackers, creating a dilemma.
visible_to: SW Virginia Sheriff's Coalition;Montgomery County Government;United Way SW Virginia

incident: Foreign State Attribution Leak Creates Diplomatic Crisis
description: A classified intelligence briefing leaked to a national newspaper attributes the cyber attack to a foreign state actor. The article reveals that the attackers specifically targeted SW Virginia's infrastructure data — environmental monitoring, supply chain logistics, and transportation networks — as part of a broader intelligence operation. Local businesses worry about being targeted again. National media descends on the region. The attribution raises questions about whether local organizations should have been warned earlier.
visible_to: VA DEQ SW Regional Office;Food City / Regional Supply Chain;VDOT SW Region
